articles.title: OpenAI CEO apologizes to Tumbler Ridge community
articles.snippet: In a letter to the residents of Tumbler Ridge, Canada, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said he is “deeply sorry” that his company failed to alert law enforcement about the suspect in a recent mass shooting.
articles.ai_summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman apologized to Tumbler Ridge residents for not notifying law enforcement about a mass shooting suspect, acknowledging the company's failure in communication.
articles.why_it_matters: Highlights accountability in tech leadership amid serious societal issues.

articles.title: Tenth Circuit Broadens CFAA ‘Loss’ Beyond Technological Harm–Moxie v. Nielsen (Guest Blog Post)
articles.snippet:
by guest blogger Kieran McCarthy After the Supreme Court’s first and only CFAA decision in Van Buren v. US in 2021, I wrote that the Court “could have done 10% more work here and provided clarity on very key questions….[but...
The post Tenth Circuit Broadens …
articles.ai_summary: The Tenth Circuit Court has expanded the definition of 'loss' under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) to include non-technological harm, following the Supreme Court's previous decision. This ruling could have significant implications for future CFAA cases and interpretations.
